title: Compute Costs of Video Generation
concept: Generating many high-resolution frames multiplies the compute cost of image diffusion many times over, making video generation far more expensive.

exercises.prompt: What do you think takes more computer power: making ONE picture, or making a VIDEO that shows 30 pictures every second?

options.text: Making a video (many pictures per second)
They take the exact same amount of power

exercises.explain: A video is really just tons of pictures (frames) shown super fast, so making a video means making WAY more pictures than just one!

cards.title: Why Is AI Video So Expensive?
cards.body: Ever wonder why AI can whip up a single cool image in seconds, but making a short AI video takes forever and costs so much more? The secret is hiding in plain sight: a video is just a LOT of images stacked together!

cards.title: Compute Cost of Video Generation
cards.body: A video is made of many single images called 'frames' shown quickly one after another. Since an AI has to generate EVERY single frame using image diffusion, and videos need dozens of frames per second, the total compute (computer power and time) needed multiplies far beyond making just one image.

cards.title: Painting One Picture vs. A Flipbook
cards.body: Imagine a painter who takes 1 hour to paint one picture. Now imagine you ask them to make a flipbook cartoon with 300 pictures, where each one is almost the same but slightly different. That's not 1 hour of work anymore — it's 300 hours! AI video generation works the same way: every frame needs its own careful 'painting' by the AI.

cards.title: Counting the Frames
cards.body: A typical video shows 24-30 frames every single second. So a short 10-second AI video might need 240-300 separate high-resolution images generated — and each of those needs the AI to run its full diffusion process, not just a quick copy-paste!

cards.title: Myth: Video Is Just One Bigger Image
cards.body: Many people think AI video generation is like making one image, just 'longer' somehow. That's not true! There is no shortcut — the AI must generate many separate high-resolution frames, and often has to also keep them looking consistent and smooth together, adding even more work on top.

cards.title: Image vs. Video: Compute Comparison
cards.body: Look at the huge jump in work required when you go from single images to full video generation.

left.text: 1 frame generated = 1 unit of compute cost. Quick and relatively cheap.

right.text: ~300 frames generated = ~300x the compute cost, plus extra work to keep frames smooth and consistent.

exercises.prompt: Why does generating a video cost much more compute than generating a single image?

options.text: Because videos need many frames, and each frame must be generated separately
Because video files are stored in a different folder
Because videos are always in black and white
Because only humans can make videos, not AI

exercises.explain: Correct! Every frame in a video is basically its own image that the AI must generate, so more frames mean much more total compute work.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A 10-second AI video only needs the AI to generate about 10 images total.
exercises.explain: False! Videos typically show 24-30 frames per second, so a 10-second video could need around 240-300 separate images, not just 10.

exercises.sentence: A video is made up of many single images called ______, shown quickly one after another.
exercises.explain: Each individual picture inside a video is called a 'frame.' Videos need tons of these frames generated to look smooth and continuous.

exercises.prompt: Which task would likely require the MOST compute power from an AI model?
exercises.explain: A 30-second video needs hundreds to thousands of individual high-resolution frames generated, making it by far the most compute-intensive task of the three.

exercises.prompt: An AI company wants to offer both image generation and video generation to users. Why should they expect video generation to need much more computing power and cost more to run?

options.text: Because video generation requires creating many high-resolution frames, each needing its own diffusion process, unlike a single image
Because video files take up more storage space on a hard drive
Because video generation uses a completely different type of electricity
Because users watch videos longer than they look at images

exercises.explain: Exactly right! Since a video is essentially dozens or hundreds of images (frames) played quickly, and each frame must go through the full image generation process, the compute cost multiplies far beyond making just one picture.
